content:
# Genesis Chapter 30 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 16v–17v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_030_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_030_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_030_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 26–27 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0030 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0031

---

*Rachel's Jealousy — She Gives Bilhah to Jacob (vv. 1–8)*

**30:1** And Rachel saw [וַתֵּרֶא רָחֵל] that she had not borne [כִּי לֹא יָלְדָה] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב] — and Rachel was jealous [וַתְּקַנֵּא רָחֵל] of her sister [בַּאֲחֹתָהּ] — and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב]: Give me [הָבָה-לִּי] children [בָנִים] — or [וְאִם-אַיִן] I shall die [מֵתָה אָנֹכִי]!

**30:2** And Jacob's anger burned [וַיִּחַר-אַף יַעֲקֹב] against Rachel [בְּרָחֵל] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Am I in the place of [הֲתַחַת] God [אֱלֹהִים אָנֹכִי] — who has withheld from you [אֲשֶׁר מָנַע מִמֵּךְ] the fruit of [פְּרִי] the womb [בָטֶן]?

**30:3** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — my maidservant [אֲמָתִי] Bilhah [בִלְהָה] — come in to her [בֹּא אֵלֶיהָ] and she shall bear [וְתֵלֵד] upon my knees [עַל-בִּרְכַּי] and I shall be built up [וְאִבָּנֶה] also through her [גַּם-אָנֹכִי מִמֶּנָּה].

**30:4** And she gave [וַתִּתֶּן-לוֹ] him [לוֹ] Bilhah [אֶת-בִּלְהָה] her maidservant [שִׁפְחָתָהּ] as a wife [לְאִשָּׁה] — and Jacob came in to her [וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ יַעֲקֹב].

**30:5** And Bilhah conceived [וַתַּהַר בִּלְהָה] and fathered [וַתֵּלֶד] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב] a son [בֵּן].

**30:6** And Rachel said [וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל]: God has judged me [דָּנַנִּי אֱלֹהִים] — and also [וְגַם] he has heard [שָׁמַע] my voice [בְּקֹלִי] and he has given me [וַיִּתֶּן-לִי] a son [בֵּן] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] she called [קָרְאָה] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Dan [דָּן].

**30:7** And Bilhah [וַתַּהַר עוֹד] conceived again [וַתַּהַר] — Rachel's maidservant [שִׁפְחַת רָחֵל] — and she fathered [וַתֵּלֶד] a second [שֵׁנִי] son [בֵּן] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב].

**30:8** And Rachel said [וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל]: Wrestlings of God [נַפְתּוּלֵי אֱלֹהִים] I have wrestled [נִפְתַּלְתִּי] with my sister [עִם-אֲחֹתִי] — also [גַּם] I have prevailed [יָכֹלְתִּי] — and she called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Naphtali [נַפְתָּלִי].

---

*Leah Gives Zilpah to Jacob — Gad and Asher (vv. 9–13)*

**30:9** And Leah saw [וַתֵּרֶא לֵאָה] that she had ceased [כִּי עָמְדָה] from bearing [מִלֶּדֶת] — and she took [וַתִּקַּח] Zilpah [אֶת-זִלְפָּה] her maidservant [שִׁפְחָתָהּ] and gave her [וַתִּתֵּן אֹתָהּ] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב] as a wife [לְאִשָּׁה].

**30:10** And Zilpah [וַתֵּלֶד זִלְפָּה] — Leah's maidservant [שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה] — fathered [וַתֵּלֶד] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב] a son [בֵּן].

**30:11** And Leah said [וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה]: Fortune has come [בָּא גָד]! — and she called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Gad [גָּד].

**30:12** And Zilpah [וַתֵּלֶד זִלְפָּה] — Leah's maidservant [שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה] — fathered [וַתֵּלֶד] a second [שֵׁנִי] son [בֵּן] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב].

**30:13** And Leah said [וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה]: In my happiness [בְּאָשְׁרִי] — for [כִּי] the daughters [בָּנוֹת] will call me happy [אִשְּׁרוּנִי] — and she called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Asher [אָשֵׁר].

---

*The Mandrakes — Leah and Rachel Bargain (vv. 14–16)*

**30:14** And Reuben went [וַיֵּלֶךְ רְאוּבֵן] in the days of [בִּימֵי] wheat harvest [קְצִיר-חִטִּים] and found [וַיִּמְצָא] mandrakes [דוּדָאִים] in the field [בַּשָּׂדֶה] and brought them [וַיָּבֵא אֹתָם] to Leah [אֶל-לֵאָה] his mother [אִמּוֹ] — and Rachel said [וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל] to Leah [אֶל-לֵאָה]: Give me now [תְּנִי-נָא לִי] some of [מִן] your son's [בְּנֵךְ] mandrakes [דוּדָאֵי].

**30:15** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר] to her [לָהּ]: Is it a small thing [הַמְעַט] that you have taken [קַחְתֵּךְ] my husband [אֶת-אִישִׁי] — and [וְ] to take also [לָקַחַת גַּם] the mandrakes [דוּדָאֵי] of my son [בְּנִי]? And Rachel said [וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל]: Therefore [לָכֵן] he shall lie with you [יִשְׁכַּב עִמָּךְ] tonight [הַלַּיְלָה] in exchange for [תַּחַת] the mandrakes [דוּדָאֵי] of your son [בְּנֵךְ].

**30:16** And Jacob came [וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב] from the field [מִן-הַשָּׂדֶה] in the evening [בָּעֶרֶב] — and Leah went out [וַתֵּצֵא לֵאָה] to meet him [לִקְרָאתוֹ] and said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: You shall come in to me [אֵלַי תָּבוֹא] — for [כִּי] I have hired you [שָׂכֹר שְׂכַרְתִּיךָ] with the mandrakes [בְּדוּדָאֵי] of my son [בְּנִי] — and he lay with her [וַיִּשְׁכַּב עִמָּהּ] that night [בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא].

---

*Births of Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah (vv. 17–21)*

**30:17** And God listened [וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים] to Leah [אֶל-לֵאָה] — and she conceived [וַתַּהַר] and fathered [וַתֵּלֶד] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב] a fifth [חֲמִישִׁי] son [בֵּן].

**30:18** And Leah said [וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה]: God has given [נָתַן אֱלֹהִים] my wages [שְׂכָרִי] — because [אֲשֶׁר] I gave [נָתַתִּי] my maidservant [אֶת-שִׁפְחָתִי] to my husband [לְאִישִׁי] — and she called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Issachar [יִשָּׂשכָר].

**30:19** And Leah conceived again [וַתַּהַר עוֹד לֵאָה] and fathered [וַתֵּלֶד] a sixth [שִׁשִּׁי] son [בֵּן] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב].

**30:20** And Leah said [וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה]: God has endowed me [זְבָדַנִי אֱלֹהִים] a good endowment [זֵבֶד טוֹב] — now [הַפַּעַם] my husband will honor me [יִזְבְּלֵנִי אִישִׁי] — for I have fathered [כִּי-יָלַדְתִּי] him [לוֹ] six [שִׁשָּׁה] sons [בָנִים] — and she called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Zebulun [זְבוּלֻן].

**30:21** And afterward [וְאַחַר] she fathered [יָלְדָה] a daughter [בַּת] and called [וַתִּקְרָא] her name [שְׁמָהּ] Dinah [דִּינָה].

---

*God Remembers Rachel — Birth of Joseph (vv. 22–24)*

**30:22** And God remembered [וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים] Rachel [אֶת-רָחֵל] — and God listened [וַיִּשְׁמַע אֵלֶיהָ אֱלֹהִים] to her — and he opened [וַיִּפְתַּח] her womb [אֶת-רַחְמָהּ].

**30:23** And she conceived [וַתַּהַר] and fathered [וַתֵּלֶד] a son [בֵּן] and said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: God has gathered away [אָסַף אֱלֹהִים] my reproach [אֶת-חֶרְפָּתִי].

**30:24** And she called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Joseph [יוֹסֵף] saying [לֵאמֹר]: May the LORD add [יֹסֵף יְהוָה] to me [לִי] another son [בֵּן אַחֵר].

---

*Jacob Asks to Leave — Wage Negotiations Begin (vv. 25–36)*

**30:25** And it was [וַיְהִי] — as soon as [כַּאֲשֶׁר] Rachel had fathered [יָלְדָה רָחֵל] Joseph [אֶת-יוֹסֵף] — that Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] to Laban [אֶל-לָבָן]: Send me away [שַׁלְּחֵנִי] that I may go [וְאֵלְכָה] to my place [אֶל-מְקוֹמִי] and to my land [וּלְאַרְצִי].

**30:26** Give [תְּנָה] my wives [אֶת-נָשַׁי] and my children [וְאֶת-יְלָדַי] — for whom [אֲשֶׁר] I have served you [עָבַדְתִּי אֹתְךָ] — and let me go [וְאֵלֵכָה] — for [כִּי] you know [אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ] my service [אֶת-עֲבֹדָתִי] that I have served you [אֲשֶׁר עֲבַדְתִּיךָ].

**30:27** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו לָבָן]: If now [אִם-נָא] I have found [מָצָאתִי] favor [חֵן] in your eyes [בְּעֵינֶיךָ] — I have divined [נִחַשְׁתִּי] and the LORD has blessed [וַיְבָרֲכֵנִי יְהוָה] me [אֹתִי] on account of you [בִּגְלָלֶךָ].

**30:28** And he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Specify [נָקְבָה] your wages [שְׂכָרְךָ] to me [עָלַי] and I will give [וְאֶתֵּנָה].

**30:29** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to him [אֵלָיו]: You yourself know [אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ] how [אֵת] I have served you [אֲשֶׁר עֲבַדְתִּיךָ] and how [וְאֵת] your livestock [מִקְנְךָ] has been [הָיָה] with me [אִתִּי].

**30:30** For [כִּי] the little [מְעַט] that was yours [אֲשֶׁר-הָיָה לְךָ] before me [לְפָנַי] has expanded greatly [וַיִּפְרֹץ לָרֹב] — and the LORD has blessed you [וַיְבָרֶךְ יְהוָה אֹתְךָ] at my foot [לְרַגְלִי] — and now [וְעַתָּה] when [מָתַי] shall I do [אֶעֱשֶׂה] also [גַּם] for my own house [לְבֵיתִי]?

**30:31** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: What [מָה] shall I give you [אֶתֶּן-לָּךְ]? And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב]: You shall not give me [לֹא-תִתֶּן-לִי] anything [מְאוּמָה] — if [אִם] you will do [תַּעֲשֶׂה] for me [לִי] this thing [הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה] — I will return [אָשׁוּב] I will pasture [אֶרְעֶה] your flock [צֹאנְךָ] [I will] keep [אֶשְׁמֹר].

**30:32** I will pass through [אֶעֱבֹר] all your flock [בְּכָל-צֹאנְךָ] today [הַיּוֹם] — removing from it [הָסֵר מִשָּׁם] every speckled [כָּל-שֶׂה נָקֹד] and spotted [וְטָלוּא] [animal] — and every dark-colored [וְכָל-שֶׂה-חוּם] among the sheep [בַּכְּשָׂבִים] — and the spotted [וְטָלוּא] and speckled [וְנָקֹד] among the goats [בָּעִזִּים] — and that shall be [וְהָיָה] my wages [שְׂכָרִי].

**30:33** And my righteousness [וְעָנְתָה-בִּי צִדְקָתִי] shall answer for me [וְעָנְתָה] in the day to come [בְּיוֹם מָחָר] — when [כִּי] you come [תָבוֹא] concerning my wages [עַל-שְׂכָרִי] before you [עַל-פָּנֶיךָ] — every [כֹּל] one that is not [אֲשֶׁר אֵינֶנּוּ] speckled [נָקֹד] and spotted [וְטָלוּא] among the goats [בָּעִזִּים] and dark [וְחוּם] among the sheep [בַּכְּשָׂבִים] — it [הוּא] shall be counted as stolen [גָּנוּב הוּא] with me [אִתִּי].

**30:34** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן]: Good [הֵן] — let it be [לוּ] according to your word [כִּדְבָרֶךָ].

**30:35** And he removed [וַיָּסַר] on that day [בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא] the striped male goats [אֶת-הַתְּיָשִׁים הָעֲקֻדִּים] and the spotted ones [וְהַטְּלֻאִים] and all the speckled female goats [וְאֵת כָּל-הָעִזִּים הַנְּקֻדּוֹת] and the spotted ones [וְהַטְּלֻאֹת] — every one [כֹּל] in which there was white [אֲשֶׁר-לָבָן בּוֹ] — and all the dark-colored ones [וְכָל-חוּם בַּכְּשָׂבִים] — and he put [וַיִּתֵּן] [them] in the hand of [בְּיַד] his sons [בָּנָיו].

**30:36** And he set [וַיָּשֶׂם] a distance of [דֶּרֶךְ] three days [שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים] between himself [בֵּינוֹ] and between Jacob [וּבֵין יַעֲקֹב] — and Jacob was pasturing [וְיַעֲקֹב רֹעֶה] the rest of [אֶת-צֹאן] Laban's [לָבָן] flock [הַנּוֹתָרֹת].

---

*Jacob's Breeding Technique — The Peeled Rods (vv. 37–43)*

**30:37** And Jacob took [וַיִּקַּח-לוֹ יַעֲקֹב] for himself rods [מַקַּל] — fresh [לַח] — of white poplar [לִבְנֶה] and of hazel [וְלוּז] and of plane tree [וְעַרְמוֹן] — and he peeled [וַיְפַצֵּל] in them [בָהֶן] white peelings [פְּצָלוֹת לְבָנֹת] — laying bare [מַחְשֹׂף] the white [הַלָּבָן] that was on the rods [אֲשֶׁר עַל-הַמַּקְלוֹת].

**30:38** And he set up [וַיַּצֵּג] the rods [אֶת-הַמַּקְלוֹת] that he had peeled [אֲשֶׁר פִּצֵּל] in the gutters [בָּרְהָטִים] — in the watering troughs [בְּשִׁקֲתוֹת הַמָּיִם] — where [אֲשֶׁר] the flocks [הַצֹּאן] came to drink [תָּבֹאן לִשְׁתּוֹת] — before [לְנֹכַח] the flocks [הַצֹּאן] — and they became warm [וַיֵּחַמְנָה] [when they came] to drink [בְּבֹאָן לִשְׁתּוֹת].

**30:39** And the flocks [וַיֶּחֱמוּ הַצֹּאן] became warm [וַיֶּחֱמוּ] at [אֶל] the rods [הַמַּקְלוֹת] and the flocks [וַתֵּלַדְןָ הַצֹּאן] fathered [וַתֵּלַדְןָ] striped [עֲקֻדִּים] [and] speckled [נְקֻדִּים] and spotted [וּטְלֻאִים].

**30:40** And Jacob separated [וַיַּפְרִד יַעֲקֹב] the lambs [אֶת-הַכְּשָׂבִים] and set [וַיִּתֵּן] the face of [פְּנֵי] the flock [הַצֹּאן] toward [אֶל] the striped [הָעָקֹד] and toward [וְכָל] all the dark-colored ones [חוּם] in Laban's flock [בְּצֹאן לָבָן] — and he put [וַיָּשֶׁת] his own flocks [לוֹ עֲדָרִים] for himself [לְבַדּוֹ] — and did not put them [וְלֹא שָׁתָם] upon [עַל] Laban's flock [צֹאן לָבָן].

**30:41** And it was [וְהָיָה] — whenever [בְּכָל-פַּעַם] the stronger flocks [הַצֹּאן הַמְקֻשָּׁרוֹת] became warm [יֶחֱמוּ] — that Jacob would put [וְשָׂם יַעֲקֹב] the rods [אֶת-הַמַּקְלוֹת] in the sight of [לְעֵינֵי] the flock [הַצֹּאן] in the gutters [בָּרְהָטִים] to cause them to become warm [לְיַחְמֵנָּה] at the rods [בַּמַּקְלוֹת].

**30:42** But when [וּבְהַעֲטִיף] the flocks [הַצֹּאן] were feeble [הָעֲטֻפוֹת] — he would not put [לֹא יָשִׂים] [them] — and so the feeble ones [וְהָיָה הָעֲטֻפִים] became [הָיוּ] Laban's [לְלָבָן] and the stronger ones [וְהַמְקֻשָּׁרִים] Jacob's [לְיַעֲקֹב].

**30:43** And the man expanded greatly [וַיִּפְרֹץ הָאִישׁ מְאֹד מְאֹד] — and he had [וַיְהִי-לוֹ] many flocks [צֹאן רַבּוֹת] and maidservants [וּשְׁפָחוֹת] and menservants [וַעֲבָדִים] and camels [וּגְמַלִּים] and donkeys [וַחֲמֹרִים].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 16v–17v:**

LC page 1 (folio 16v, GEN_030_page_001.jpg) is photographed inverted (rotated 180°), consistent with the West Semitic Research Project's archiving method for this folio — as noted in the Genesis 29 manuscript notes. Once re-oriented, the page carries the close of Genesis 29 in the upper portion and the opening of Genesis 30 through approximately verse 16, covering Rachel's jealousy, the births of Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher through the handmaids, and the mandrakes episode. The three-column Sephardic square script is fully vocalized and cantillated throughout. A parashah setumah marker (ס) is visible at the close of chapter 29 before chapter 30 begins, confirming the traditional parasha boundary. The name יוֹסֵף (Joseph) is legible with its full vocalization in v. 24, and the divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) appears clearly with Qere vocalization markings in vv. 24 and 27.

LC page 2 (folio 17r, GEN_030_page_002.jpg) is in standard orientation. It carries Genesis 30:17–30:38 approximately, covering the births of Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah; God's remembering Rachel; Joseph's birth; Jacob's wage negotiations with Laban; and the beginning of the peeled-rod technique. The peeled-rod passage (vv. 37–38) shows the distinctive botanical vocabulary — לִבְנֶה (white poplar), לוּז (hazel/almond), עַרְמוֹן (plane tree) — fully vocalized and legible. The chapter marker is visible in the masorah.

LC page 3 (folio 17v, GEN_030_page_003.jpg) is photographed inverted (rotated 180°), as is standard for this folio's archival photography. Once re-oriented, it carries the conclusion of Genesis 30 (vv. 39–43) and the opening verses of Genesis 31. The climactic expansion formula — וַיִּפְרֹץ הָאִישׁ מְאֹד מְאֹד (v. 43, "and the man expanded greatly, greatly") — is legible with its doubled מְאֹד and the masorah parva notations in the margin. The list of Jacob's acquired wealth (flocks, maidservants, menservants, camels, donkeys) closes the chapter.

Codex Vaticanus page 26 (online viewer 0030, GEN_030_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the portion from late Genesis 29 through the middle of Genesis 30, with the margin notation *CAP. XXXII* visible on the left. The three-column uncial text carries the births of Dan through Asher and into the mandrakes episode, all legible in the characteristic Vaticanus hand. Page 27 (online viewer 0031, GEN_030_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the second half of chapter 30 through the wage negotiation, peeled rods, and the chapter's close, with the margin notation *CAP. XXXIII* beginning on the right column, marking the new Greek chapter division.

---

**30:1 — "Give me children, or I shall die" (*havah-lî vanîm wĕ'im-'ayin mētāh 'ānōkhî*, הָבָה-לִּי בָנִים וְאִם-אַיִן מֵתָה אָנֹכִי):**
Rachel's cry is structurally a demand followed by a conditional death-threat. The imperative *havāh* (הָבָה, "give!") is emphatic. The second clause — *wĕ'im-'ayin mētāh 'ānōkhî* — is literally "and if there is nothing [i.e., if you give me nothing], dead am I" — using the predicate adjective *mētāh* (מֵתָה, feminine participle of *mût*, "to die") placed before the pronoun for emphasis: "dead — I [will be]." The LXX Vaticanus reads *dos moi tekna, ei de mē, teleutēsō egō* (δός μοι τέκνα, εἰ δὲ μή, τελευτήσω ἐγώ) — "give me children, and if not, I shall die" — using the future indicative *teleutēsō* (I shall die), preserving the death-threat but softening it slightly from an immediate statement of dying to a future prediction. The Hebrew participle *mētāh* is starkly present-tense in force. EMET renders it "I shall die" as the most idiomatic English equivalent of the ominous Hebrew present-as-future.

---

**30:2 — "Am I in the place of God?" (*hatachat Elohim anokhi*, הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנֹכִי):**
Jacob's response is the only instance in Genesis where a patriarch speaks with manifest anger (*wayyihar-'ap*, וַיִּחַר-אַף — "and his anger burned") in response to a wife's request. The rhetorical question *hatachat Elohim anokhi* uses the particle *ha-* as an interrogative: "Am I in the place of God — who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?" The divine name here is אֱלֹהִים (*Elohim*), not YHWH — Jacob attributes Rachel's barrenness to the sovereign will of God, not to any failure of his own. The LXX Vaticanus reads *mē antì Theou egō eimi* (μὴ ἀντὶ Θεοῦ ἐγώ εἰμι) — "am I in the place of God?" — preserving the rhetorical question exactly.

---

**30:3 — "She shall bear upon my knees" (*'al-birkkay*, עַל-בִּרְכַּי) — surrogacy idiom:**
The phrase *yālad 'al-birkkay* ("to give birth upon [someone's] knees") is the legal-social formula for surrogate motherhood in the ancient Near East. The child born by the handmaid is placed on the knees (*birkkayim*) of the primary wife, symbolically transferring maternity — the child is then "hers." Parallel usage appears in Genesis 50:23 (Joseph sees his grandchildren "born upon his knees" as an adoptive gesture). Rachel's statement *wĕ'ibbāneh gam-'ānōkhî mimmennāh* (וְאִבָּנֶה גַּם-אָנֹכִי מִמֶּנָּה) — "and I shall be built up also through her" — uses the verb *bānāh* (בָּנָה, "to build") in the Niphal passive — a significant pun: *bānāh* is the root of both "to build" and *bēn* ("son"). To "be built up" (*'ibbāneh*) through a son is a wordplay on the fundamental structure of family-building in Hebrew thought (cf. the similar pun in Gen 16:2, where Sarai uses the same phrase of Hagar). The LXX Vaticanus reads *teknōpoiēsomai kai egō ex autēs* (τεκνοποιήσομαι καὶ ἐγώ ἐξ αὐτῆς) — "I shall also bear children through her" — rendering the *'ibbāneh* idiom with the straightforward Greek word for child-bearing rather than preserving the "built up" metaphor.

---

**30:6 — Dan (*Dān*, דָּן) — naming etymology:**
Rachel's naming speech for Dan: *dānannî Elohim* (דָּנַנִּי אֱלֹהִים) — "God has judged me / vindicated me." The verb *dān* (דָּן, Qal perfect of *dîn*, "to judge, to plead one's cause, to vindicate") gives the name *Dān* ("Judge" or "Vindicated"). The name encapsulates Rachel's sense that God has finally heard her plea and rendered a verdict in her favor. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ekrine moi ho Theos* (ἔκρινέ μοι ὁ Θεός) — "God has judged for me" — and *Dān* is transliterated as *Dan* (Δάν). EMET renders it "Dan" per project convention for English name forms.

---

**30:8 — Naphtali (*Naphtalî*, נַפְתָּלִי) — naming etymology and "wrestlings of God":**
The naming speech is among the most linguistically dense in Genesis. *Naphtuley 'Elohim niphtalti 'im-'ăkhotî* (נַפְתּוּלֵי אֱלֹהִים נִפְתַּלְתִּי עִם-אֲחֹתִי) — "wrestlings of God I have wrestled with my sister." The noun *naphtuley* (נַפְתּוּלֵי, plural construct from *pātal*, "to twist, to wrestle, to be tortuous") intensifies the verbal idea: "divine wrestlings" or "God-wrestlings" — using *Elohim* as an intensifying superlative (as in "a great city before God" = "an exceedingly great city"), meaning "mighty wrestlings" or "wrestlings of the utmost intensity." The Niphal perfect *niphtalti* (נִפְתַּלְתִּי) from the same root: "I have been twisted / I have wrestled." The name *Naphtalî* = "my wrestling." This verse is the only place in Genesis where *pātal* is used before the famous *'ābaq* wrestling in Gen 32 — the Jacob-at-Jabbok episode — making Rachel's naming speech a verbal anticipation of her husband's own divine wrestling. Rachel has not been wrestling God, but her sister — the competition for children is the wrestling match. EMET preserves the literal "wrestlings of God" for *naphtuley 'Elohim*, following the MT. The LXX Vaticanus reads *synelabomēn meta tēs adelphēs mou* (συνελαβόμην μετὰ τῆς ἀδελφῆς μου) — "I have contended/wrestled with my sister" — and transliterates the name as *Nephthalim* (Νεφθαλίμ).

---

**30:11 — Gad (*Gād*, גָּד) — Qere/Ketiv variant:**
The naming of Gad presents one of the most discussed Qere/Ketiv variants in the Pentateuch. The Leningrad Codex records a textual divergence at this verse:

- **Ketiv** (what is written): בָּגָד (*bāgad*) — which could be read as a form of the verb *bāgad* (בָּגַד, "to betray, to deal treacherously") — a perplexing reading that makes no straightforward sense as a birth exclamation.
- **Qere** (what is to be read): בָּא גָד (*bā' gād*) — "Fortune has come!" — with *gād* meaning "fortune, luck" (a Semitic deity-name as well as a common noun for good fortune). The Qere reading is clearly the contextually appropriate exclamation at the birth of a son and is supported by the LXX Vaticanus, which reads *en tychē* (ἐν τύχῃ) — "in/with fortune/luck."

The masorah of the Leningrad Codex marks this as a Qere/Ketiv pair, and the Qere *bā' gād* has virtually universal support from the ancient versions. EMET renders the Qere reading — "Fortune has come!" — as the translation, and notes the Ketiv בָּגָד here. The name *Gād* = "Fortune" (also the name of a god of fortune in the ancient Semitic world; cf. Isa 65:11 where Gad is explicitly a deity-name). The tribe of Gad inherits the name in its tribal usage stripped of any deity association.

---

**30:13 — Asher (*'Āshēr*, אָשֵׁר) — naming etymology:**
The naming speech uses two forms of the root *'āshar* (אָשַׁר, "to be happy, blessed, fortunate"): *bĕ'āshrî* (בְּאָשְׁרִי, "in my happiness/blessedness") and *'ishshĕrûnî* (אִשְּׁרוּנִי, Piel imperfect — "they will call me happy/blessed"). The name *'Āshēr* = "Happy / Blessed one." The same root is used in the Psalter's beatitudes (*'ashrê* — "blessed is the one who...") and throughout Proverbs. The LXX Vaticanus reads *en makarismō mou* (ἐν μακαρισμῷ μου) — "in my blessedness" — and transliterates the name as *Asēr* (Ἀσήρ).

---

**30:14 — Mandrakes (*dûdā'îm*, דּוּדָאִים):**
The *dûdā'îm* (singular *dûdā'*, perhaps from *dôd* — "beloved, love") are the mandrake plant (*Mandragora officinarum*), a Mediterranean herb with aromatic yellow fruit. In the ancient world, mandrakes were associated with love, fertility, and even aphrodisiac properties — a belief reflected in the etymological connection to *dôd* ("love") and attested in Song of Songs 7:14 (*haddûdā'îm nātnû rêach* — "the mandrakes gave forth fragrance"). The dramatic irony of the mandrakes episode is multilayered: Leah trades the mandrakes (the fertility charm) to Rachel for a night with Jacob, but it is Leah — not Rachel — who conceives as a result of that night (v. 17). The fertility-charm works for the one who gave it up, not the one who craved it. God's sovereign action (v. 17: "God listened to Leah") overrides the folk-magic of the mandrakes. EMET renders *dûdā'îm* as "mandrakes" throughout, noting this as a botanical-cultural term. The LXX Vaticanus reads *mandragoroi* (μανδραγόρου) — the standard Greek term for mandrakes.

---

**30:18 — Issachar (*Yiśśākhār*, יִשָּׂשכָר) — naming etymology:**
The name *Yiśśākhār* is built on two possible etymological connections, both present in Leah's speech:

1. **"There is wages/reward"**: From *yesh* (יֵשׁ, "there is") + *sākhār* (שָׂכָר, "wages, reward") — "there is a wage."
2. **"Man of wages"**: Some read the name as *'îsh śākhār* — "man of hire/reward" — connecting to the mandrakes transaction itself: Leah "hired" Jacob for the night with the mandrakes and received a son as her reward. Leah's speech makes the connection explicit: *nātan Elohim śĕkhārî* (נָתַן אֱלֹהִים שְׂכָרִי) — "God has given my wages/reward — because I gave my maidservant to my husband." The LXX Vaticanus reads *apedōken ho Theos ton misthon mou* (ἀπέδωκεν ὁ Θεὸς τὸν μισθόν μου) — "God has paid back my wages" — and transliterates the name as *Issachar* (Ἰσσαχάρ).

The unusual doubled שׂ in the MT spelling יִשָּׂשכָר (Issaschar) reflects the doubling of the consonant in the name's etymology or a scribal preservation of an archaic spelling. EMET renders the name as "Issachar" per standard English convention.

---

**30:20 — Zebulun (*Zĕvulûn*, זְבוּלֻן) — naming etymology:**
The naming speech for Zebulun presents two interlocked etymological plays:

1. **"Endowed"**: *zĕvadanî 'Elohim zēved tôv* (זְבָדַנִי אֱלֹהִים זֵבֶד טוֹב) — "God has endowed me a good endowment" — from the root *zāvad* (זָבַד, "to endow, to give a gift"). The noun *zēved* = "endowment, gift."
2. **"Honor / dwell with"**: *yizbĕlēnî 'îshî* (יִזְבְּלֵנִי אִישִׁי) — "my husband will honor me / dwell with me" — from the root *zāval* (זָבַל, "to honor, to exalt" or "to dwell"). The name *Zĕvulûn* is most closely related to this second root: "the honored one" or "dwelling."

Two different roots (*zāvad* and *zāval*) are both evoked in a single naming speech, an unusual double pun even by the standards of these birth-narratives. The LXX Vaticanus reads *hairetiei me ho anēr mou* (αἱρετιεῖ με ὁ ἀνήρ μου) — "my husband will prefer/choose me" — for *yizbĕlēnî*, taking the word in the sense of "exalt/prefer" and transliterating the name as *Zaboulon* (Ζαβουλών).

---

**30:21 — Dinah (*Dînāh*, דִּינָה) — no naming speech:**
Dinah's birth is recorded without a naming speech — a pointed and unusual silence compared with the twelve elaborate etymological namings of her brothers. The name *Dînāh* is from the same root as Dan (*dîn*, "to judge") and means "judgment" or "vindicated." No explanation is offered; no mother exclaims over her birth. The brevity — *wĕ'akhar yāldāh bat wayyiqrā' 'et-shĕmāh Dînāh* ("and afterward she bore a daughter and called her name Dinah") — is starkly minimal. Dinah will play a central narrative role in Genesis 34. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Dina* (Δεινά).

---

**30:22–24 — Joseph (*Yôsēph*, יוֹסֵף) — naming etymology and dual wordplay:**
Rachel's naming of Joseph contains a double wordplay:

1. **"He removed" (*'āsaph*, אָסַף)**: Rachel first says *'āsaph 'Elohim 'et-cherpātî* (אָסַף אֱלֹהִים אֶת-חֶרְפָּתִי) — "God has gathered away my reproach" — from the root *'āsaph* ("to gather, to take away, to remove"). God has removed the reproach of barrenness.
2. **"May he add" (*yôsēph*, יֹסֵף)**: Then: *yōsēph YHWH lî bēn 'akhēr* (יֹסֵף יְהוָה לִי בֵּן אַחֵר) — "may the LORD add to me another son" — from the root *yāsaph* (יָסַף, "to add, to increase"). The name *Yôsēph* captures both the backward-looking *'āsaph* ("taken away" = the reproach is gone) and the forward-looking *yāsaph* ("may he add" = the prayer for yet another son). This anticipatory prayer for "another son" is fulfilled in Genesis 35:16–18 with the birth of Benjamin.

Notably, the divine name switches from *Elohim* (v. 22, 23 — "God remembered Rachel... God opened her womb... God has gathered away my reproach") to *YHWH* (v. 24 — "may the LORD add to me another son"). The shift from the general divine name to the covenant name YHWH in Rachel's prayer for Joseph is significant: in her moment of greatest personal fulfillment, Rachel addresses the LORD by his covenant name. EMET renders *Elohim* as "God" and *YHWH* as "the LORD" per project conventions throughout. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Iōsēph* (Ἰωσήφ).

---

**30:27 — "I have divined" (*nikhashti*, נִחַשְׁתִּי):**
Laban's statement *nikhashti* (נִחַשְׁתִּי, Piel perfect of *nākhash*, "to divine, to practice divination, to observe omens") is remarkable. Laban says he has divined (*nikhashti*) and learned that the LORD has blessed him on account of Jacob. The verb *nākhash* is the same root used for the serpent (*nākhāsh*, נָחָשׁ, Gen 3:1) — perhaps a deliberate resonance — and for the divination practices explicitly forbidden in Leviticus 19:26 and Deuteronomy 18:10. Laban is portrayed as a practitioner of omen-divination, which is entirely consistent with his characterization as an Aramean who keeps household idols (*terāphîm*, Gen 31:19). He does not attribute his knowledge to YHWH's direct revelation but to his own divination practice. Yet paradoxically, what his divination showed him was the blessing of YHWH. The LXX Vaticanus reads *oiōnisamēn* (οἰωνισάμην) — "I have divined by bird-omens" — using the Greek term for augury (from *oiōnos*, "bird of omen"), a legitimate rendering of the general divination concept. EMET renders it "I have divined" to preserve the technical force of *nikhashti*.

---

**30:30 — "At my foot" (*lĕraglî*, לְרַגְלִי):**
The phrase *lĕraglî* (literally "at my foot" or "at my feet") is an idiom meaning "in my wake, wherever I walked, on my account." Jacob says the LORD has blessed Laban *lĕraglî* — "at my foot" — i.e., wherever Jacob has been, God's blessing has accompanied him and spilled over to Laban. The same idiom appears in 2 Samuel 15:16 and elsewhere with the sense of "in the train of, accompanying." EMET renders it "at my foot" to preserve the literal idiom. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eph' hois eisēlthon pros se* (ἐφ' οἷς εἰσῆλθον πρός σε) — "for everything with which I came to you" — a contextual paraphrase rather than a literal rendering.

---

**30:32–33 — The Speckled Flock Agreement — vocabulary of markings:**
The passage uses a precise technical vocabulary for animal markings:
- *nāqōd* (נָקֹד) — "speckled" — having irregular spots or dots
- *tālû'* / *tālō'* (טָלוּא / טָלֻא) — "spotted, patchy" — having large irregular patches of color
- *'āqōd* (עָקֹד) — "striped, streaked" — having bands or stripes (used for the male goats in v. 35)
- *khûm* (חוּם) — "dark-colored, brown, black" — used specifically for the sheep (which were normally white)

These four terms are used with precision to describe different patterns of non-uniform coloring on goats and sheep. Jacob's proposal takes as his wages all the non-uniformly colored animals — which, in a normal flock, would be a minority. Laban agrees, thinking he is keeping the majority of solid-colored animals. The extraordinary breeding results that follow (vv. 37–42) overturn this calculation. EMET preserves the distinct Hebrew terms — speckled, spotted, striped/streaked, dark-colored — throughout, matching each term to its specific Hebrew word.

---

**30:37–42 — The Peeled Rods — folk biology and divine providence:**
Jacob's technique of placing peeled, stripped rods in the watering troughs where the animals mated, so that they would look at the rods during conception and produce striped, speckled offspring, reflects ancient Near Eastern folk-biological beliefs about prenatal influence through the maternal visual experience during conception. The same theory appears in other ancient literature. Modern biology makes clear that this technique cannot work genetically — animals' visual experience during mating has no effect on the coloration of offspring. The narrative resolution comes in Genesis 31:10–12, where Jacob tells Rachel that God showed him in a dream that the striped male goats were mating with the speckled and spotted females — meaning that God, not the rods, was directing the genetics of the flock. The peeled rods are thus presented not as an effective folk-magic technique but as Jacob's action running alongside God's sovereign blessing. EMET renders the vocabulary of the rods passage faithfully:

- *maqal* (מַקַּל) — "rod, staff, stick"
- *libneh* (לִבְנֶה) — "white poplar" (Populus alba) — with the pun that *libneh* echoes *lāvān* ("white"), the same root as Laban's name (*Lāvān* = "white")
- *lûz* (לוּז) — "hazel" or "almond tree" — this is a contested identification; Vulgate reads *amygdali* (almond), LXX reads *karyas* (walnut/hazel)
- *'armôn* (עַרְמוֹן) — "plane tree" (Platanus orientalis, the Oriental plane) — a large, distinctive tree common in the Levant
- *pĕṣalôt lĕvānôt* (פְּצָלוֹת לְבָנֹת) — "white peelings" — the white inner wood exposed by peeling
- *rĕhāṭîm* (רְהָטִים) — "gutters, channels, troughs" — the water channels or troughs for the animals
- *shiqătôt hammayim* (שִׁקֲתוֹת הַמָּיִם) — "watering troughs" — the drinking troughs

The LXX Vaticanus at v. 37 reads *rhabdous chloras* (ῥάβδους χλωράς) — "fresh/green rods" — for *maqal lach* (מַקַּל לַח), and *karyas* (καρύας) — "walnut/hazel" — for *lûz*, and *platānou* (πλατάνου) — "plane tree" — for *'armôn*, confirming the identification of the plane tree. The LXX uses *rachtēr* (ῥαχτήρ, a loanword) for *rĕhāṭîm* (gutters/troughs).

---

**30:41–42 — "The stronger" and "the feeble" — *mĕqushsharôt* and *'ăṭuphôt*:**
The terms Jacob uses to distinguish between the two groups of animals at mating time are:
- *mĕqushsharôt* (הַמְקֻשָּׁרוֹת, Pual participle of *qāshar*, "to bind, to be strong, to be robust") — "the strong, robust ones"
- *'ăṭuphôt* (הָעֲטֻפוֹת, Qal passive participle of *'āṭaph*, "to be faint, feeble, covered") — "the feeble, weak ones"

Jacob uses the rods only during the matings of the stronger animals, ensuring that the stronger offspring would be his (striped/speckled) and the weaker would be Laban's (solid-colored). This is a deliberate selective breeding strategy — Jacob is not merely relying on the visual trick but also timing it for maximum effect. EMET renders these as "the stronger flocks" and "the feeble ones" to capture the literal force of the contrasting adjectives.

---

**30:43 — "And the man expanded greatly, greatly" (*wayyiphrotz hā'îsh mĕ'ōd mĕ'ōd*, וַיִּפְרֹץ הָאִישׁ מְאֹד מְאֹד):**
The verb *pārats* (פָּרַץ, "to break out, to burst forth, to expand") with the doubled intensifier *mĕ'ōd mĕ'ōd* ("very, very much / exceedingly greatly") marks the climactic closing statement of the chapter with rhetorical force. This same verb *pārats* was used by Laban in v. 30 (*wayyiphrotz lārōv*, "expanded greatly") to describe how Jacob's presence caused Laban's flocks to multiply — now the verb turns back upon Jacob himself, as the one who has expanded. The doubling of *mĕ'ōd* (מְאֹד מְאֹד) is emphatic superlative: not merely "greatly" but "greatly, greatly" — exceedingly, beyond measure. This is the same rhetorical pattern used for God's evaluation of creation in Gen 1:31 (*ṭôv mĕ'ōd*, "very good") — but doubled. EMET preserves the doubled *mĕ'ōd* with "greatly, greatly" rather than collapsing it to the single "exceedingly."

The inventory of Jacob's wealth — flocks, maidservants, menservants, camels, donkeys — mirrors the conventional list of patriarchal wealth (cf. Gen 12:16, 24:35). The ordering puts the human servants before the animals, a slight reversal of the typical Abrahamic wealth formula, emphasizing that Jacob's gain included the full range of household assets. The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai diesteilatho ho anthrōpos sphodra sphodra* (καὶ διεστείλατο ὁ ἄνθρωπος σφόδρα σφόδρα) — "and the man expanded greatly greatly" — preserving the doubled *sphodra* for *mĕ'ōd mĕ'ōd* exactly.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 26–27):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 26–27 carry Genesis 30 across two pages in three-column uncial. The margin of page 26 carries the notation *CAP. XXXII* and page 27 carries the notations *CAP. XXXIII* and *CAP. XXXIIII*, reflecting the ancient Greek chapter division system.

Key LXX readings for Genesis 30:

1. **30:1** — LXX *dos moi tekna* — "give me children" (using *tekna*, "children," for *bānîm*, "sons/children"). The specificity of the Hebrew *bānîm* (male plural) is slightly generalized in the LXX.

2. **30:6** — *Dan* transliterated as *Dan* (Δάν); *ekrine moi ho Theos* for *dānannî Elohim* — "God judged for me" — preserving the judicial force of the Dan-etymology.

3. **30:8** — LXX *synelabomēn* (I have wrestled/contended together) for *niphtalti* — a slightly different nuance from the Hebrew "wrestling" but capturing the sense of contest. The LXX rendering of *naphtuley Elohim* as implied "divine contention" (God-level intensity of wrestling) is present.

4. **30:11** — LXX *en tychē* (ἐν τύχῃ, "in fortune/luck") for the Qere *bā' gād* — confirming the Qere reading "Fortune has come." This is the clearest ancient support for the Qere over the Ketiv (*bāgad* = "betrayal/treachery"), which makes no contextual sense.

5. **30:13** — *Asēr* (Ἀσήρ) transliterated; *makaria egō hoti* for *bĕ'āshrî kî* — "blessed/happy am I, because."

6. **30:14** — *mandragoroi* (μανδραγόρου) for *dûdā'îm* — the standard Greek botanical term for mandrakes.

7. **30:20** — *Zaboulon* (Ζαβουλών); *hairetiei me* (αἱρετιεῖ) for *yizbĕlēnî* — "will choose/prefer me" — taking *zāval* in the sense of preferring rather than honoring/dwelling.

8. **30:22** — *emnēsthē ho Theos tēs Rachēl* — "God remembered Rachel" — exact rendering.

9. **30:27** — *oiōnisamēn* (ὠιωνισάμην) — "I divined by bird-omen" — for *nikhashti* — the specific Greek term for augury/divination, preserving the technical force of the Hebrew divination vocabulary.

10. **30:37** — Botanical vocabulary: *karyas* (walnut/hazel) for *lûz*, *platānou* (plane tree) for *'armôn*, *libanōtou* or *leukas* for *libneh* (white/poplar) — the LXX identifications provide ancient Near Eastern corroboration for the tree identifications.

11. **30:43** — LXX *sphodra sphodra* (σφόδρα σφόδρα) — "greatly greatly" — the doubled superlative exactly mirroring the MT's doubled *mĕ'ōd mĕ'ōd*.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 30 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary) are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.
